title: Building the Theme Switcher
summary: How I got a light/dark toggle working that actually remembers your choice across pages.
content: This was the first real bit of JavaScript I added to the site. The idea is simple, it's a button in the nav flips the whole site between light and dark. Clicking the toggle sets a data-theme attribute on the body, and my CSS does the actual color swapping through custom properties. I already had all my colors as variables in :root, so the dark theme is just a second block that overrides those same variables under [data-theme='dark']. To make the choice stick I save it to localStorage and read it back on every page load, falling back to light if nothing's saved. The part that tripped me up came later. Once I moved the nav into a header that loads dynamically, the toggle button didn't exist yet when my script first ran, so clicking it did nothing. I had to pull the setup into its own named function and call it again after the header finishes loading. Lesson learned, you can't wire up a button that isn't in the DOM yet.

title: Loading the Header and Footer Dynamically
summary: Getting rid of six copies of the same nav and footer using fetch().
content: Before this I was copy-pasting the exact same nav and footer into all six pages, which meant every time I changed one link I had to change it in six spots. So I moved the markup into components/header.html and components/footer.html and wrote a little loadComponent function that fetches each file and drops it into a placeholder div with innerHTML. That being said, I ran into two issues. First, fetch() doesn't work if you just open the HTML file directly in the browser - it needs an actual server, so I had to run python3 -m http.server to test it. Second, my theme toggle broke, because the button now gets loaded in after the rest of the page, so I had to re-run the theme setup right after the header injects. Once I sorted those two out it felt really clean - the nav lives in one file now and every page just pulls it in.

title: Rendering the Blog from a JSON File
summary: Pulling posts out of a JSON file and building the cards in JavaScript instead of hardcoding them.
content: This post is coming out of a JSON file. Instead of writing each blog entry in HTML, I keep them all in data/posts.json and let js/blog.js fetch that file and build a card for each one. Every post has a title, date, category, summary, and content, and the script loops over them and creates the markup on the fly, including a Read More button that toggles the full content. I added a couple of extra touches. The posts get sorted newest-to-oldest by subtracting their Date objects, and the most recent one gets a Latest Post badge. The annoying bug was the date. My dates are stored like 2026-07-18, and when I formatted them they kept showing up one day early. Turns out a plain date string gets parsed as UTC midnight, and since my timezone is behind UTC it rolled back a day. Passing timeZone UTC into toLocaleDateString fixed it. That one took me a while to even notice was happening.

title: Client-Side Form Validation
summary: Making the contact form check what you typed before it pretends to send anything.
content: The contact form actually checks your input now before doing anything. When you hit submit I stop the default form submission and validate three things: the name can't be empty, the email has to match a basic email pattern, and the message has to be at least 20 characters. If something's off I show a small red error message right under that field, and it clears the moment you start fixing it. I set that up with an input event listener on each field. If everything passes, I swap the whole form out for a green success message, since I don't have a real backend to send to yet. The email validation was the trickiest part. I didn't want anything overcomplicated, just a check that there's some text, an @, and a domain with a dot after it. What I like about this one is how much more finished the form feels now that it talks back to you instead of silently doing nothing when you click send.
